target.label: Price Close
target.recommended_reason: Raw levels are useful for replaying a published point path, but they often collapse into near-persistence equations.

holistic_equation.honesty_note: Holistic equation renders Euclid's deterministic law as one symbolic expression and attaches the selected probabilistic lane as an explicit stochastic rule.

artifacts.narrative: The artifacts page is about traceability. The workspace root is /Users/danielbloom/Desktop/euclid/build/workbench/20260418T180827Z-gld-price-close, which is the folder holding this run. Inside it, analysis.json is the core analysis output that ties together the page-level results. The dataset used by the workbench is stored at datasets/gld-price-close.csv, while the raw source history is stored in fmp-history.json. The point page connects back to manifests/operator-point.yaml, which records the point-lane setup. The benchmark page connects back to benchmark-root/reports/predictive_generalization/fmp_gld_benchmark.md, which is the saved benchmark report. These file paths do not add new evidence beyond the page summaries, but they show exactly where each part of the run was saved.
artifacts.summary: All saved outputs live under the workspace root /Users/danielbloom/Desktop/euclid/build/workbench/20260418T180827Z-gld-price-close. The main files map cleanly to the pages: analysis.json is the run record, gld-price-close.csv is the dataset used for the workbench, fmp-history.json is the raw history file, operator-point.yaml is the point-lane manifest, and the markdown file under benchmark-root/reports is the benchmark report.

cautions: Benchmark-local winner and operator publication are separate lanes.
The descriptive fit should not be read as a strong structural explanation when it barely beats the naive last-value baseline.

benchmark.headline: The benchmark-local winner offers a descriptive fit, but it does not override the operator lane’s abstention.
key_takeaways: A benchmark-local descriptive fit was available for this run.
The winner was analytic_backend / analytic_lag1_affine, not the last-observation runner-up.
The winner’s code length was 6,970 total code bits versus 7,013 for the runner-up.
The fitted equation was effectively a persistence rule with a tiny intercept.
The operator publication status remained abstained despite the benchmark-local winner.
benchmark.narrative: The benchmark page exists to compare candidates in a broader benchmark-local search, not to declare that the operator lane published something. The descriptive fit is available and comes from the benchmark-local winner, identified as analytic_backend / analytic_lag1_affine. The runner-up was algorithmic_search_backend / algorithmic_last_observation. The winner had 6,970 total code bits, while the runner-up had 7,013, and selection used the stated ordering: minimum total code bits, then maximum description gain, then minimum structure code bits, then minimum canonical byte length, then candidate id. The resulting equation was y(t) = -0.160657 + 1.00169*y(t-1). The semantic audit labels this near_persistence and gives the delta form Δy(t) = -0.160657 + 0.00169*y(t-1). Numerically, it only reduced one-step MAE from 2.011155 to 1.997742, a relative improvement of about 0.7% versus y(t) = y(t-1). The benchmark page is also explicit that the descriptive fit is not an operator publication. It even recommends Daily Return as a better default target for interpretable descriptive equations, because that would make the role of level persistence more obvious instead of hiding it inside raw prices. So this page provides a descriptive comparison, not a published operator forecast rule.
benchmark.summary: The benchmark page says a descriptive fit is available, and the winner was analytic_backend / analytic_lag1_affine. Its equation was y(t) = -0.160657 + 1.00169*y(t-1). That benchmark-local winner was selected over a runner-up last-observation candidate by a code-length rule, with 6,970 total code bits versus 7,013. But this remains separate from operator publication, which still abstained.

terms.meaning: The candidate that won inside the benchmark comparison process for this run.

A fit that behaves almost the same as simply carrying forward the previous value.

cautions: This target is the raw close level, and the run digest says raw levels usually reward persistence and drift.

overview.headline: This run saw a strong rise in the series, but the main fitted story is still just “today looks a lot like yesterday.”
key_takeaways: The series increased from 165.89 to 445.93 over the sample, a gain of 280.04.
The benchmark-local descriptive fit was y(t) = -0.160657 + 1.00169*y(t-1), which is very close to “use yesterday’s value.”
That fit beat the naive last-value baseline by only about 0.7% in one-step MAE.
The operator point lane abstained because of robustness_failed and perturbation_protocol_failed.
All 4 probabilistic lanes completed and passed calibration, but their evidence strength needs careful reading on the probabilistic page.
overview.narrative: The observed series rose a lot over the full sample: from 165.89 at the start to 445.93 at the end, with a minimum of 151.23, a maximum of 495.9, an average of 227.21, and a standard deviation of 81.17. That means the run was trying to predict a level that both drifted upward and tended to persist from one day to the next. The descriptive equation shown on the overview page captures that exactly: y(t) = -0.160657 + 1.00169*y(t-1). In plain English, it says the next close is almost just the previous close, with only a tiny offset. The semantic audit explicitly labels this as near persistence and rewrites it as Δy(t) = -0.160657 + 0.00169*y(t-1). The practical gain was very small: MAE of 1.997742 instead of 2.011155 for a naive last-value rule, only a 0.7% improvement. So the run’s main caution is that raw close levels naturally reward persistence, which can make an equation look meaningful when it is mostly just following the level. Separately, the operator lane did not publish a point model even though a replay verified a candidate, because publication gates were not cleared. On the probabilistic side, four lanes completed and all four passed calibration, but those pages still need to be read carefully alongside their evidence notes.
overview.summary: Across 1,256 rows from 2021-04-19 to 2026-04-17, the close moved from 165.89 to 445.93, up 280.04 or about 168.8%. The benchmark-local descriptive fit was y(t) = -0.160657 + 1.00169*y(t-1), which is effectively a persistence rule with a tiny adjustment. That fit only improved one-step MAE from 2.0112 to 1.9977 versus simply using the last value, so it should be read as level tracking, not as a deep market explanation. The operator point lane verified a candidate on replay but still abstained because it failed robustness and perturbation checks.

terms.meaning: A pattern where the next value is very similar to the previous value.
Mean absolute error; the average size of forecast mistakes, ignoring direction.
A result that cleared the system’s publication gates, not just a candidate that was found or replayed.

cautions: Do not treat the point-lane equation as an approved forecasting rule; the lane abstained.

point.headline: The point lane found a simple upward-drift candidate, but it was not published.
key_takeaways: The selected point-lane family was drift.
The fitted point equation was y(t) = 166.48 + 0.218356*t.
The point search only explored constant, drift, linear_trend, and seasonal_naive.
Publication status was abstained, not published.
The abstention reasons were robustness_failed and perturbation_protocol_failed.
point.narrative: On the point page, the search was limited to the current-release surface: constant, drift, linear_trend, and seasonal_naive. Within that limited menu, the selected family was drift. Its equation, y(t) = 166.48 + 0.218356*t, describes the level as a baseline of 166.48 plus about 0.218356 units for each time step. That is a very simple trend description. The key issue is not whether a candidate existed; the page explicitly says operator replay verified one. The issue is publication status. The point lane still abstained, with reason codes robustness_failed and perturbation_protocol_failed. So the correct reading is: a simple trend-like candidate was found, but it did not survive the checks needed to publish it.
point.summary: The operator point lane selected the drift family from a narrow search surface and produced y(t) = 166.48 + 0.218356*t. That means a straight-line rise over time rather than a richer structure. Even though operator replay verified a candidate, the lane abstained from publication because it failed robustness and perturbation checks.

terms.meaning: A simple model that moves in a steady average direction over time.
The candidate did not hold up well enough under the system’s stability checks.
A test that checks whether a result remains acceptable when the setup is slightly disturbed or stressed.

cautions: A calibration pass here does not by itself show strong real-world robustness.
Single-candidate templates mean these lanes did not win a broad internal competition.
The evidence note on every lane is the main thing to remember: only 21 realized forecasts across 1 origin.
probabilistic.headline: All four probabilistic lanes passed calibration, but each one comes with very limited realized-forecast evidence.
key_takeaways: All 4 probabilistic lanes completed.
All 4 probabilistic lanes passed calibration.
Each lane explicitly warns that there were only 21 realized forecasts across 1 origin.
Each lane ran as a single-candidate template rather than a broader search.
The distribution and event-probability equations were both near-persistence style rules.
probabilistic.narrative: The probabilistic page is positive on completion status: 4 lanes completed and 4 passed calibration. But the page also gives a strong warning about how much weight to put on those passes. For every lane, the evidence headline says: only 21 realized forecasts across 1 origin, and calibration can pass here without proving robustness. The lane setups were also narrow. The distribution lane only allowed analytic_lag1_affine and produced y(t) = -0.243542 + 1.002099*y(t-1), again very close to a persistence rule. The event probability lane only allowed algorithmic_last_observation and used y(t) = y(t-1), which is the plain last-value rule. The interval lane only allowed recursive_level_smoother, and the quantile lane only allowed analytic_lag1_affine; those pages did not list equations. So the main message is not that the probabilistic system found a wide set of competing, battle-tested models. It is that each lane completed, calibration formally passed, and the evidence behind that pass was thin in practice because it came from just 21 realized forecasts at a single origin.
probabilistic.summary: The distribution, event probability, interval, and quantile lanes all completed and all passed calibration. But each lane’s evidence headline says there were only 21 realized forecasts across 1 origin, so a pass here does not prove strong robustness. Also, each lane ran as a single-candidate template, meaning there was no broad model competition inside these lanes.

terms.meaning: Whether the probabilistic outputs line up reasonably with what actually happened.
